# III. The Capitalist Class

## 1. Trade and Credit

```{=html}
<p class="fst">We have seen how the mass of the population in the countries with a capitalist mode of production becomes more and more a mass of <em>proletarians</em>, of workers who are separated from their means of production, so that they can produce nothing on their own account and are therefore compelled, if they are not to starve, to sell the only thing they possess, their <em>labour-power.</em> The majority of the peasants and small tradespeople in fact already belong to the proletariat as well. What distinguishes them from it, their property, is now only a thin curtain, more suited to conceal their exploitation and dependence than to prevent it, a curtain that every stronger breeze lifts and carries away. On the other side we see a small band of the propertied, capitalists and large landowners, to whom alone the most important means of production, the most important sources of life for the entire population, belong, and to whom this sole possession lends the possibility and the power to make the propertyless dependent on them and to exploit them.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>While the majority of the population sinks ever deeper into want and misery, it is this small band of capitalists and large landowners which, together with the parasites clinging to it, alone reaps all those enormous advantages that arise from the achievements of present-day civilisation, above all from the progress in the natural sciences and their practical application.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Let us now look more closely at this small band of the chosen; let us consider the role it plays in economic life and the consequences that arise from it for society.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We have already become acquainted with the three types into which capital falls: <em>merchant capital,</em> <em>usurer's capital,</em> and <em>industrial capital.</em> The last-named type of capital is the youngest, perhaps not as many <em>centuries</em> old as the other two types of capital count <em>millennia.</em> But the youngest brother has grown faster, much faster than the elder ones; he has become the giant who has subjugated them and forced them into his service. For the small-scale enterprise in its perfect (classical) form, trade is not an absolute necessity. The peasant, like the artisan, can obtain the means of production, insofar as he has to buy any, directly from the producer; he can sell his product directly to the consumer. At this stage of economic development, trade serves chiefly luxury, but it is not indispensable for the progress of production as a whole, for the maintenance of society.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Capitalist production, on the other hand, is, as we have seen, from the very outset dependent on trade – just as, on the other hand, trade from a certain stage onward requires capitalist production for its further development. The more the latter spreads, the more the capitalist mode of production becomes the dominant one, the more necessary the progress of trade proves to be for the whole of economic life. Today it no longer serves merely abundance, luxury. The entire production, indeed the feeding of the population of a capitalist country, today depends on trade moving undisturbed in its channels. This is one of the reasons that must at present make a world war far more devastating than it has ever been. War leads to a stagnation of trade, but such a stagnation today means a stagnation of production, of the whole of economic life, means an economic ruin that extends further and is no less calamitous than the devastations in the theatre of war.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Just as important as the development of <em>trade</em> for the capitalist mode of production has become the development of <em>usury.</em> Under the rule of the small-scale enterprise the usurer was a parasite who exploited the distress or the frivolity of others in order to draw off their blood. The money that he lent to others served as a rule – and the rule was that every producer himself possessed the necessary means of production – only for purposes of unproductive expenditure. If, for example, a nobleman borrowed money, he did so in order to squander it; if a peasant did so, it was to pay his money dues or the costs of a lawsuit with it. Borrowing at interest was therefore regarded as immoral and was generally condemned.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Quite otherwise in the capitalist mode of production. Money is now a means of establishing a capitalist enterprise, of buying and exploiting labour-power. When an entrepreneur nowadays takes up money in order to found a new enterprise or to expand an already existing one, this does not mean – assuming of course that the enterprise prospers – that he diminishes his previous income by the amount of the interest on this sum of money. Rather, the borrowed money serves him to exploit labour-power, and thus to increase his income, and indeed by more than the amount of the interest owed. Usury now loses its original character. Its role as a means of exploiting distress and frivolity recedes more and more in the face of the role of "fertilising" capitalist production, that is, of making it possible that its development proceeds still more rapidly than would be made possible by the mere accumulation of capital in the money-chests of the industrial capitalists. The abhorrence of the usurer now ceases; he becomes spotless and also receives a new, euphonious name: <em>credit-giver.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>At the same time the main direction of the movement of interest-bearing capital has become another one. The sums of money which the usurer-capitalists heaped up in their chests formerly flowed out of these reservoirs through a thousand channels to the non-capitalists. Today the money-chests of usurer's capital – of the credit institutions – have rather become reservoirs into which, through a thousand channels, the money of the non-capitalists flows in, in order to be conveyed from there to the capitalists. Credit is today, as of old, a means of making non-capitalists – the propertyless and the propertied – liable to pay interest to capital. But it has now also become a powerful means of transforming the possessions that are found in the hands of the various classes of non-capitalists, from the enormous riches of the Catholic Church and of the old nobility down to the meagre savings-pennies of servant-girls and day-labourers, into capital, that is, into a means of exploiting the one and of decomposing the other of these classes. People praise the present-day credit institutions, the savings-banks, etc., because they turn the savings-pennies of the wage labourers, artisans and peasants into capital and turn these into "capitalists," as the adherents of the present order maintain. But this accumulation of the money of non-capitalists has no other purpose than to place new capitals at the disposal of the capitalists and thereby to accelerate the development of the capitalist mode of production. But what that means for wage labourers, peasants and artisans, we have seen.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>If the credit institutions of today increasingly ensure that the entire wealth of the various classes of non-capitalists is turned into capital that is placed at the disposal of the capitalist class, they ensure on the other hand that the capital of the capitalist class is put to better use than before. They become the collecting reservoirs of all the sums of money belonging to the individual capitalists, which the latter have at any given moment no opportunity to employ in their own enterprises, and they make these sums, which would otherwise lie there "dead", accessible to other capitalists who need them. They also make it possible to convert commodities into money before they have been sold, and thereby reduce the time of circulation, and hence also the amount of capital that is required at any given moment to operate a particular enterprise.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>By all this the amount and the effective force of the capital that stands at the disposal of the capitalist class is enormously expanded. Credit has therefore become today one of the most powerful levers of capitalist production. Alongside the high development of machinery and the accumulation of the industrial reserve army, it is one of the chief causes of that rapid force of the present-day mode of production which enables industry to shoot up quickly at the slightest impulse and to expand mightily.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But credit is still far more sensitive to every disturbance than trade. And every shock it undergoes propagates itself throughout the whole of economic life.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Some economists have regarded credit as a means by which the propertyless or those of little property might be made into capitalists. But, as its very name indicates, credit rests on the confidence of the creditor in the debtor. The more the latter possesses, the greater the security he offers, and the greater the credit he enjoys. The credit system is thus only a means of procuring for the capitalists even more capital than they possess, of increasing the preponderance of the capitalists, of sharpening the social antagonisms, not of softening them.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The credit system is accordingly not only a means of developing capitalist production more rapidly and of enabling it to exploit every favourable conjuncture; it is also a means of promoting the downfall of small-scale enterprise; but it is finally also a means of making the whole machinery of the present-day mode of production ever more complicated and more sensitive to disturbances, and of carrying the feeling of insecurity into the ranks of the capitalists as well, of making the ground on which they move ever more unsteady.<br/> <a id="ch3-t2" name="ch3-t2"> </a></p>
```

## 2. Division of Labour and Competition

```{=html}
<p class="fst">While economic development leads, on the one hand, to bringing commerce and credit into ever closer relation with industry, it brings about, on the other hand, that as a consequence of the increasing division of labour the various functions (activities) which the capitalist has to fulfil in economic life fall more and more to different, mutually separate enterprises and institutions. In former times the merchant had not merely to buy and sell the commodities; he had also to collect them, store them and bring them to the often very distant market; he had to sort the commodities, display them and make them accessible to the individual buyers. Today we have not merely the division of labour between retail trade and wholesale trade; we have special large enterprises for the transport system and for the storage of commodities (warehouses, elevators); on the largest central markets, the <em>stock exchanges,</em> buying and selling has become so much an activity in itself, so detached from the other activities of the merchant, that there one not merely buys and sells commodities that are still far away, indeed have not yet even been produced, but that one also buys commodities without wishing to take possession of them, that one sells commodities one does not possess.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In former times one could not imagine a capitalist without a large money-safe in which the money he took in accumulated, from which he took the money he needed for his payments. Today the cash-handling of the capitalists in the economically advanced countries, notably England, America, has become the affair of special enterprises, <em>banks.</em> One no longer pays the capitalist, but his bank, and one receives from it, not from the capitalist, what the latter owes. Thus it comes about that a few central enterprises handle the cash affairs of the entire capitalist class of a country.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But when in this way the various functions of the capitalist fall to different independent enterprises, they thereby become independent of one another only outwardly, juridically; economically they remain, as before, chained together in the closest manner and dependent on one another. The functions of one of these enterprises cannot proceed properly if the functions of any of the other enterprises with which it is connected in business suffer a disturbance.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The more commerce, credit and industry become mutually dependent on one another, and the more the various activities of the capitalist class fall to separate enterprises, the greater the dependence of the individual capitalist upon the others. The capitalist economy of a country – indeed, in certain respects already that of the whole world market – becomes more and more a single immense body whose parts stand in the closest connection with one another. If the mass of the population falls ever more into dependence on the capitalists, so these fall ever more into dependence on one another.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The economic machinery of the present-day mode of production becomes more and more so intricate and sensitive a mechanism that its undisturbed progress depends more and more on all its countless little wheels meshing precisely with one another and doing their duty. Never has a mode of production so much required planned regulation as the present one. But private property makes it impossible to bring plan and order into this machinery. While the individual enterprises become <em>economically</em> ever more dependent on one another, they remain legally, <em>juridically</em> independent of one another. The operating means of every individual enterprise are private property, and their owner can dispose of them at his pleasure.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The more large-scale enterprise develops, the larger the individual enterprises become, the more the economic activity within each of them becomes a regulated one, ordered down to the smallest detail according to a definite, carefully considered plan. But the joint working of the individual enterprises with one another is left to the blind driving force of <em>free competition.</em> With an immense squandering of energy and means and through ever stronger convulsions, this keeps the economic machinery in motion; not by placing everyone in his right place, but by crushing everyone who stands in the way of the progress of the machinery. This is called the “selection of the best in the struggle for existence.” In truth, however, free competition eliminates less the incapable than rather those who stand in a wrong place, for the maintenance of which either their abilities or else – and this is the main thing – their <em>capital</em> is insufficient. But nowadays it is not content merely to eliminate those not equal to the “struggle for existence.” Every such elimination of a defeated one drags with it the ruin or the shaking of numerous other existences that stood in economic connection with the enterprise that has fallen to bankruptcy – wage labourers, creditors, suppliers, etc.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Today people still like to use the proverb: everyone is the smith of his own fortune. It stems from the age of small-scale enterprise, where the fate of the individual worker depended on his personal qualities – and only his own and that of his family. Today the fate of every member of a capitalist society depends ever less on his personality, ever more, on the contrary, on a thousand kinds of circumstances over which he has no influence. It is no longer a selection of the best that competition brings about today.<br/> <a id="ch3-t3" name="ch3-t3"> </a></p>
```

## 3. Profit

```{=html}
<p class="fst">Whence, then, does the capitalist class derive its income? <em>Commercial capital</em> and <em>usurer's capital</em> originally obtain their gains and interest through deductions from the <em>property</em> of persons of the most varied classes who depend on their assistance or mediation. <em>Industrial capital</em> hatches its profit through the exploitation of the <em>propertyless</em> wage labourers. But the more the capitalist mode of production develops, the more industrial capital outstrips the other kinds of capital, the more it makes them subservient to itself, as we have seen — which, however, it can only do by ceding to them a portion of the surplus value it has drawn from the wage labourers. As a result of this development, the surplus value produced by the proletarians becomes more and more the sole source from which the <em>entire capitalist class</em> draws its revenues. Just as handicraft and peasant agriculture are in the process of disappearing and influence the essence of present-day society less and less, so too the old forms of merchant and usurer's capital, which drew their gains from the exploitation of the non-capitalist classes. There already exist today states without handicraft and peasantry — see England. But not a single one of the modern states is conceivable without large-scale industry. Whoever wishes to understand the present-day forms of capital must proceed from industrial capital: in the surplus value which capitalist industry produces is to be sought the most essential and ever more prominent of the sources from which all capitalist gain flows.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We have already become acquainted, in the previous chapter, with the surplus value which the industrial proletarian produces and the industrial capitalist appropriates. We have also seen in what way the quantity of surplus value which the individual worker produces is increased more and more in proportion to the size of his wage: through the increase of the worker's burden of labour, the introduction of labour-saving machines and cheaper labour-power, and so forth. At the same time, with the development of capitalist industry, the number of exploited proletarians also grows: thus the mass of surplus value flowing to the capitalist class swells more and more.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But alas, alas, "life's unalloyed joy falls to no mortal's lot," and so the capitalist class must "share," however hateful sharing may be to it; it must share the surplus value it has snatched with <em>landed property</em> and the <em>state.</em> And the share which these two pocket grows from year to year.<br/> <a id="ch3-t4" name="ch3-t4"> </a></p>
```

## 4. Ground Rent

```{=html}
<p class="fst">When we speak of the classes that increasingly become the sole owners and exploiters, the monopolists of the means of production, we must distinguish between the <em>capitalists</em> and the <em>large landowners.</em> For land is a means of production of a peculiar kind. It is the <em>most indispensable</em> of all; without it no human activity is possible; even the seafarer and the aeronaut require a place of departure and a place of landing. But land is also a means of production that <em>can by no means be increased at will.</em> Yet it has hitherto scarcely ever happened in any larger region that every scrap of earth has been taken into cultivation by its inhabitants. Even in China there are still vast stretches of uncultivated land.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Under the rule of the small-scale peasant enterprise in medieval Europe, every peasant possessed his own farmstead and his own arable land. Water, forest and pasture were common property, and the quantity of uncultivated land was so great that anyone could be permitted to take into possession and cultivate those plots which he made arable in the wilderness. Then came the development of commodity production with its consequences, which we have already come to know. The products of the soil became commodities, they acquired a value. This retroactively made the soil too into a commodity with a value. The individual peasant communities and cooperatives now sought to close the circle of their members, and these began to regard the land which they possessed in common and which they in part (as forest and pasture) also cultivated in common, no longer as the inalienable common property of the community or cooperative, but as a kind of common <em>private property</em> belonging only to the present members and their heirs, from which all community members joining later were excluded. They wanted to make the land their <em>monopoly.</em> But someone else too became covetous of the community's common property, namely the <em>landlord,</em> who had been the protector of the common property; if this landed property, which had now become so valuable, was to become private property, then it should be <em>his</em> private property. In most regions, but especially where large-scale agriculture developed, the landlords succeeded in seizing the peasant common property. The clearing of the peasants, the driving of individual peasants from their holdings, followed thereafter. All land, including that not used agriculturally, now passed into private property; landed property became the privilege of a few.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Thus, through economic development, and especially through the formation of large landed property, land became a monopoly long before the available area of cultivation was exhausted, long before one could yet have spoken of an overpopulation. If, therefore, land occupies an exceptional position as a means of production because it cannot be increased at will, this is not because all existing land has already been <em>taken into cultivation,</em> but because it has already – at least in the civilised countries – been completely <em>taken into possession</em> by a minority. Thereby a monopoly of a quite peculiar kind arises. The capitalist class does indeed have, as against the propertyless classes, the monopoly on the means of production. But within the capitalist class itself there is no monopoly of particular members of it over particular means of production, at least no lasting monopoly. If a capitalist ring forms to monopolise a certain, highly important invention, e.g. a new machine, other capitalists can always be found who either likewise buy this machine, or trump it by means of a new invention, or sooner or later imitate it. All this is not possible in the case of landed property. The landowners have a monopoly not only as against the propertyless classes, but also as against the capitalist class.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The peculiarity of landed property is most sharply developed in England, where a small number of families holds the landed property of the whole country in their hands and clings to it, does not sell it. Whoever needs land receives it from them merely on <em>loan</em> against a certain rent for tenure, the <em>ground rent.</em> <a href="#ch3-n7" id="ch3-f7" name="ch3-f7">[7]</a> A capitalist who wants to build a factory or a dwelling house, to open a mine or to undertake an agricultural enterprise, cannot as a rule buy the land in England, but can only lease it.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>With us the capitalist is mostly also a landowner: the factory owner possesses the ground on which his factory stands, the mine operator is the owner of the pits which he has worked; on the other hand, the large landowner is mostly also an industrial capitalist – agriculture, as already mentioned, in the capitalist mode of production also becomes a capitalist industry, which by and large is subject to the same laws as the other branches of industry. If the capitalist farms on his own land, if he is himself a landowner, he naturally does not need to share the surplus value with the latter. But this changes nothing essential in the matter. For he has become a landowner only by having paid the former owner of the plot a capital whose interest corresponds to the amount of the ground rent. He therefore pays the ground rent in any case; in the one form as in the other it diminishes his profit.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The monopoly character of landed property becomes all the more acute, however, the stronger the demand for landed property grows, the more the population increases, the more the capitalist class requires landed property, the more the capitalist mode of production develops. To the same degree grows also the <em>ground rent,</em> that is, the amount of the <em>total</em> ground rent paid in capitalist society. It is by no means necessary that the rent of every plot should grow. A plot, other things being equal, yields the more rent the more fertile and the more favourably situated (e.g. nearer to or farther from the market) it is – we cannot of course enter here into the laws of rent themselves. – The opening up of new fertile lands can therefore depress the ground rent of an exhausted soil; but the ground rent of the newly opened soil will grow all the more strongly. Similarly, improvements in the means of transport can depress the rent of a region situated near the market in favour of regions lying farther away. Both have taken place in the last two decades. American ground rent has risen, and indeed, in so far as agricultural protective tariffs did not counteract it, at the expense of the West European. Yet this holds only for agriculturally used land. In the towns, ground rent is everywhere in a steady, rapid rise. For the capitalist mode of production crowds the mass of the population ever more together in the towns. Unfortunately, it is not so much the profit of the industrial capitalists that suffers from this as rather the physical and mental health of the poorer classes of the people. Here, in the <em>housing question,</em> we come to know a new source of the sufferings of the proletariat. This is not the place to enter into it more closely.<br/> <a id="ch3-t5" name="ch3-t5"> </a></p>
```

## 5. Taxes

```{=html}
<p class="fst">If the landowner curtails the capitalist's share of the surplus value to an ever greater extent – either directly or indirectly, for example by raising the maintenance costs of the workers –, then the <em>state</em> is no less active in the same manner. The modern state has grown great with the capitalist class and through it, just as, on the other hand, it has been the most powerful means of raising that class up. The two have mutually promoted one another. The capitalist class cannot do without the state. It requires its protection both at home and abroad.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The more the capitalist mode of production develops, the more abrupt become the antagonisms and contradictions it engenders; but the more intricate too becomes its mechanism, the greater the dependence of individuals upon one another, the greater the need for an authority standing above them and ensuring that everyone fulfils the duties arising from his economic functions. So sensitive a mechanism as the present-day mode of production can tolerate less than any other of the previous modes of production the settling of antagonisms and disputes through the self-help of those concerned. In place of self-help comes the law guarded by the state.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Capitalist exploitation is by no means the product of a particular legal system. It is rather its needs that have created the law prevailing today and brought it to dominance. That law does not produce exploitation; it merely ensures that this, like other processes in economic life, proceeds as smoothly as possible. If we have just described <em>competition</em> as the <em>driving force</em> of the present-day mode of production, then we may regard the <em>law</em> as the <em>lubricating oil</em> which ensures that the frictional resistances in the economic mechanism make themselves felt as little as possible. The more these frictional resistances grow, the more abrupt on the one hand the antagonisms between exploiters and exploited, between the propertied and the propertyless become, the larger in particular the lumpenproletariat; the more, on the other hand, within the capitalist class each individual entrepreneur, for the undisturbed continuation of his enterprise, depends upon the prompt cooperation of numerous other capitalists, the stronger the need for a suitable body of law, the more extensive the recourse to its organs – <em>the judiciary</em> and <em>the police –,</em> the greater the need for a strong state power capable of giving force to the law.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But it is not merely a matter for the capitalists of being able to produce, buy and sell undisturbed within their own country. Foreign trade plays a great role in capitalist production from the very outset, and the more this becomes the dominant mode, the more the securing and extension of the foreign market appears as a vital interest of the whole nation. But on the world market the capitalists of one nation encounter competitors from other nations. In order to face these down, they call upon the state, which through its military power is to secure respect for their rights or – what is still better – to drive the foreign competitors away altogether. Just as the states and monarchs fall ever more into dependence upon the capitalist class, so too the war armies serve ever more not merely the personal purposes of the monarchs, but also the purposes of the capitalist class. Wars turn ever more from dynastic wars into <em>commercial wars</em> and finally into <em>national</em> wars, which in the last instance are likewise to be traced back only to the economic antagonisms between the capitalist classes of the individual nations.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The capitalist state therefore requires not only an extensive <em>army of officials</em> for the purposes of the judiciary and the police (as well as, of course, for the administration of its finances), but also a strong <em>war army.</em> Both armies are in steady growth in capitalist states; but in the most recent times the war army has grown more rapidly than the army of officials.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>As long as the application of science in industrial technology had not made its entry, military technology too changed only slowly. But as soon as machinery had attained dominance in industry and set it in constant upheaval, the previous stability came to an end for the war machines as well. Every day brings a new invention and discovery which, scarcely tested and introduced at great cost, is again displaced by another revolutionising innovation. And the war machines become ever more extensive, more complicated and more costly. At the same time the advances in transport make it possible to bring ever larger masses of troops to the theatre of war; as a result the armies are extended ever further.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Under these circumstances the state expenditures for military purposes (to which most state debts are also to be reckoned) have, in all the great European states, been in a downright insane increase over the last twenty years.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The state becomes ever more costly, its burdens ever more oppressive. The capitalists and great landowners naturally seek, wherever they hold the handle of legislation in their hands, to shift the burdens as much as possible onto the other classes of the people. But there is less and less to be had from those classes, and so, despite all the tricks of the exploiter gentlemen, their surplus value too must, by dint of the state, be curtailed ever more.<br/> <a id="ch3-t6" name="ch3-t6"> </a></p>
```

## 6. The Fall of Profit

```{=html}
<p class="fst">Simultaneously with the development just described, the quantity of total capital that the capitalist class invests "productively" in its various enterprises shows the tendency to grow more rapidly than the exploitation of the working class, more rapidly than the mass of surplus value created by it.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We cannot here go more closely into the causes of this phenomenon, the understanding of which presupposes greater economic knowledge. An example may serve to illustrate what has been said.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Let us take a case that is quite striking. Let us compare a hand spinner of a hundred years ago, who was perhaps exploited by a capitalist as a domestic industrial worker, with a machine spinner of today. How much capital is necessary to enable the latter to perform his work! And how small, by contrast, was the capital that the capitalist employed in hand-spinning: he paid the spinner his wages and gave him the cotton or the flax to spin. With regard to the wage not much has changed, but the machine spinner today consumes perhaps a hundred times as much raw material as the hand spinner; and what enormous buildings, steam engines, spinning machines, etc., are necessary if the machine-spinning is to be able to proceed!</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Yet another circumstance comes into consideration; the capitalist of a hundred years ago who employed the spinner laid out in his enterprise merely the outlays for <em>wages</em> and <em>raw materials</em>; there was hardly any fixed capital, the spinning wheel not being worth reckoning. His capital turned over rapidly, let us say in a quarter of a year; he therefore needed to lay out, to advance, in his enterprise only a <em>quarter</em> of the capital that he employed in the whole year. Today, in a machine-spinning establishment, the amount of capital that has to be laid out in machinery and buildings is uncommonly high. The turnover time of the sum of capital advanced for wages and raw materials may be the same as a hundred years ago, but the turnover time of the other portion of capital, which a hundred years ago scarcely existed, is a very long one.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>A series of causes works in the opposite direction: for example the credit system, but above all the fall in the value of products, which is a necessary consequence of the increase in the productivity of labour. But these causes are by no means capable of wholly abolishing the development in question. This development takes place in all branches of industry, in some more slowly, in others more rapidly, and has the effect that the magnitude of the annually advanced sum of capital which in general falls to the head of each worker in industry grows rapidly and considerably.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Let us assume that a hundred years ago this sum of capital amounted to 100 marks and that today it has grown to 1000 marks; let us further assume that the exploitation of the worker has <em>quintupled;</em> if the surplus value he produced a hundred years ago amounted to 50 marks annually, then today, with the same annual wage, it amounts to 250 marks. The <em>mass</em> of surplus value produced has in this case therefore in and of itself <em>(absolutely)</em> risen <em>enormously;</em> but in <em>proportion</em> to the <em>quantity of capital</em> that the capitalist lays out annually, the surplus value has <em>fallen;</em> a hundred years ago this proportion amounted to 50 percent, today it amounts to only 25 percent.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This is of course only an example; but the tendency it illustrates really exists.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The <em>total quantity</em> of surplus value produced annually in a capitalist country is in steady and rapid growth; but still more rapidly grows the total quantity of the capitalist class's capital laid out in the various capitalist enterprises, over which the surplus value is distributed. If one considers besides this that, as we have just seen, state burdens and ground rent take away more and more from the mass of annually produced surplus value, then one will comprehend that the quantity of surplus value which on average in the year falls to a <em>definite sum of capital</em> is in steady <em>decline,</em> <em>although the exploitation of the worker increases.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Profit, that is to say that part of surplus value which remains to the capitalist entrepreneur, therefore shows the tendency to fall in proportion to the total capital advanced by him, or, expressed differently, in the course of the development of the capitalist mode of production the profit which a definite sum of capital yields generally becomes ever smaller. Naturally this holds only for the average over larger periods of time. One indication of this fall is the steady fall in the rate of interest.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p><em>While, therefore, the exploitation of the worker has the tendency to rise, the rate of the capitalist's profit shows the tendency to fall.</em> This is one of the strangest contradictions of the capitalist mode of production, which is so rich in contradictions.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>From this fall it has been concluded that capitalist exploitation will one day come to an end of itself. Capital would ultimately yield so little profit that the capitalists would go about hungry seeking employment. But that would hold only if the rate of profit fell steadily while the quantity of total capital remained the same. This, however, is by no means the case. The quantity of total capital in the capitalist nations grows more rapidly than the rate of profit declines. The increase of capital is one of the preconditions of the fall in the rate of profit, and when the rate of interest sinks from 5 to 4 and from 4 to 3 percent, this does not diminish the income of that capitalist whose capital has in the meantime risen from one million to two and four millions.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The fall in the rate of profit or of the rate of interest by no means signifies a diminution of the income of the capitalist class; for the <em>mass</em> of surplus value that flows to it steadily increases; this fall merely reduces the income of those capitalists who are not in a position to enlarge the extent of their capital correspondingly. In the course of economic development the limit rises ever higher from which a capital begins to maintain its owner "in a manner befitting his station." Ever greater grows the sum of wealth of which one needs at the very least in order to be able to live off the labour of others without one's own labour. What fifty years ago was still a considerable fortune has today become a trifle.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The fall in profit and the rate of interest brings about not the downfall but only the <em>narrowing</em> of the capitalist class. Every year small capitalists are eliminated from it and subjected to the same death struggle as artisans, small traders and small peasants; a death struggle which may last a shorter or a longer time, but which ultimately ends for them or their children with downfall into the proletariat. What they attempt in order to escape their fate for the most part only furthers their ruin.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>One is astonished at the number of foolish people whom every swindler today knows how to bring to the point of entrusting him with their money, if only he promises them a really high rate of interest for it. As a rule, people are not so foolish as they appear; the fraudulent enterprise is the last straw at which they clutch in order to obtain a corresponding return on their small fortune. It is less greed than the fear of want that makes them blind.<br/> <a id="ch3-t7" name="ch3-t7"> </a></p>
```

## 7. The Growth of Large-Scale Enterprises. The Cartels

```{=html}
<p class="fst">Alongside the competitive struggle between the artisan trade and large-scale capitalist industry, there rages the competitive struggle between larger and smaller capitalist enterprises. Every moment brings a new invention, a new discovery, whose application considerably raises the productivity of labour. Each of these advances devalues, to a greater or lesser degree, the existing plant and equipment and brings with it the necessity of new acquisitions, and often also of enterprise expansions. Whoever does not possess the necessary capital for this becomes, sooner or later, incapable of competing and goes under, or is driven to turn with his capital to a branch of industry in which a smaller enterprise is not yet incapable of competing. Thus the competition in large-scale industry promotes overcrowding in the sphere of small-scale industry and in this manner works to ruin the artisan trade even in the few branches of industry in which the small-scale enterprise is still to some extent viable.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Ever larger, ever more extensive grow the enterprises of large-scale industry. From large-scale enterprises that count their workers by the hundreds they become giant enterprises that employ thousands of workers (spinning mills, breweries, sugar factories, ironworks, etc.). More and more the smaller enterprises disappear: from a certain point onward, industrial development leads not to an increase but to a continual <em>decrease</em> in the number of enterprises of large-scale capitalist industry.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But that is not all. Economic development also leads to the result that ever more capitalist enterprises are united in <em>one hand</em> – whether in the possession of a single capitalist or of a capitalist cooperative, which, however, is economically likewise only <em>one</em> person (a juridical person).</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The paths to this are indeed manifold.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>One is opened up by the endeavour of the capitalists to exclude competition. We came to know competition above as the driving force of the present-day mode of production. It is the driving force of commodity production and of commodity trade in general. But however necessary competition is for the totality of the society of commodity production, every commodity owner nonetheless craves to see <em>his</em> commodity on the market without competition. If he is the sole owner of a sought-after commodity, if he has a <em>monopoly</em> on it, then he can raise its price far above its value, and then those who desire this commodity are in the most complete dependence upon him. Where several sellers of commodities of the same kind appear on the market, they can artificially create a monopoly by combining with one another, so that together they form a single seller. Such a combination, a <em>cartel</em> or ring, trust, syndicate, etc., is of course all the more readily possible the smaller the number of competitors whose conflicting interests are to be reconciled.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Insofar as the capitalist mode of production widens the market and the number of competitors upon it, it makes the formation of monopolies in trade and in industry more difficult. But in every capitalist branch of enterprise there comes, as already mentioned, sooner or later the moment from which onward its further development leads to a decrease in the number of enterprises it contains. From that point on it ripens more and more toward cartelisation. The moment of ripeness can be hastened in a given country by the fact that its internal market is preserved from foreign competition by protective tariffs. The number of competitors for this market is thereby diminished, and it is made easier for the domestic producers to combine, to create a monopoly, and, thanks to the “protection of national labour,” to fleece the national consumer over the ears.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In the last twenty years the number of cartels, by means of which the production and price of certain commodities are “regulated,” has, as is well known, grown greatly, especially in the countries of protective tariffs – the United States, Germany, France. Where cartelisation occurs, the various enterprises that combine actually form only <em>one</em> enterprise under <em>one</em> management; very often they are also formally placed under a unified management.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is precisely the most important commodities, the most indispensable for the progress of production, <em>coal</em> and <em>iron,</em> whose production or processing is most readily subject to cartelisation. Most cartels extend their effects far beyond the branches of industry monopolised by them; they make the whole machinery of production dependent upon a few monopolists.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Simultaneously with the endeavour to combine the various enterprises of a particular branch of industry in one hand, there develops the endeavour to unite in a single one various enterprises of different branches of industry, of which one supplies the raw material or the tools for the other. Many railway companies possess their own coal mines and locomotive factories; the sugar factories strive to grow themselves a part of the sugar beet they process; the potato producer sets up his own schnapps distillery, etc.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Yet a third path of combining various undertakings in one hand is to be named. It is the simplest of all.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We have seen that the capitalist in the present-day mode of production has to attend to highly important tasks, functions. However superfluous these may be under another organisation of production, under the domination of commodity production and of private property in the means of production, large-scale production is only possible as capitalist production. It is there necessary, if production is to proceed and if the products are to reach the consumers, that the capitalist intervene with his capital and apply it in a manner corresponding to the purpose. Even though the capitalist produces nothing, even though he creates no value, he nonetheless plays an important role in the economic machinery.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But the larger a capitalist enterprise becomes, the more necessary it is for the capitalist to hand over a part of his growing burden of business, either to other capitalist undertakings, as we have seen, or to his own salaried officials, whom he engages so that they may perform his functions. Whether these functions are attended to by a wage labourer or by a capitalist naturally makes no difference in economic life; they do not become value-creating because the capitalist has others perform them. The capitalist must therefore pay for them out of the surplus value. We come to know here a new deduction from the surplus value, whereby profit is likewise diminished. The capitalist naturally reckons the expenditures for his officials, directors, administrators, etc., among the costs of production, among <em>wages.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>If the growth of an enterprise compels the capitalist in question to relieve himself by engaging officials, the growth of the surplus value makes this expenditure easier for him. The greater the surplus value, the more of his functions the capitalist can have performed by officials, until finally he has rid himself of his entire activity, so that only the concern remains to him of how he is to invest profitably that part of his profit which he does not consume.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The number of enterprises in which matters have gone this far increases from year to year. This is shown most clearly by the growth of <em>joint-stock companies,</em> in which, recognisable even to the dimmest eye, the <em>person</em> of the capitalist has already, in the present mode of production, become entirely a matter of indifference, and only his <em>capital</em> is still of significance. In England (older figures are lacking for Germany) the number of registered joint-stock companies in 1845 was still only 57 (apart from those subsequently dissolved), in 1862 only 1,815, whereas in 1888 it was already 11,001, with a paid-up share capital of over 12,000 million marks.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>People have believed that in the joint-stock system a means had been found to make the advantages of large-scale enterprise accessible to the "little people." But like credit, the joint-stock system too, which indeed forms only a particular form of credit, is rather a means of placing the fortunes of the little people at the disposal of the great capitalists.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Once the person of the capitalist has become dispensable in an enterprise, then anyone can run it who possesses the necessary capital, whether he understands anything of the particular business or not. This creates for a capitalist the possibility of uniting in his hands the most diverse enterprises, which bear no relation whatever to one another. Most conveniently the great capitalist gets joint-stock companies into his hands. He needs only to possess a larger fraction of their shares – which are as quickly bought as sold – in order to make the enterprise dependent upon himself and subservient to his interests.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Finally, it must also be noted that in general large capitals grow faster than small ones; for the greater the capital, the greater (other conditions being equal) the mass of profit, hence the income (revenue) it yields; the smaller the fraction of it which the capitalist requires for his personal use, the greater the portion which he can add (accumulate) as new capital to his existing capital. A capitalist whose enterprise yields 10,000 marks a year will, according to capitalist notions, be able to live only modestly on it. He can be glad if he succeeds in being able to set aside 2,000 marks a year – <em>one fifth</em> of his profit. The capitalist whose capital is so large that he draws 100,000 marks of income from it can, even if he spends some five times as much on himself and his family as the first-mentioned capitalist, nevertheless add <em>three fifths</em> of his profit to his capital. And if a capitalist's capital is so very large that it yields a million a year, then he will have trouble spending a tenth of it on himself, if he is a normal person, and despite great expenditure will comfortably be able to accumulate <em>nine tenths</em> of his profit.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>While the small capitalists must struggle ever harder for their existence, the great fortunes swell ever more rapidly and within a short time attain an enormous extent.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>If we sum all this up: the growth in the size of enterprises, the rapid swelling of the great fortunes; the diminution in the number of enterprises, the increasing concentration of several enterprises in a single hand, then it becomes clear that the tendency of the capitalist mode of production is to unite the means of production, which have become the monopoly of the capitalist class, in ever fewer and fewer hands. This development finally amounts to this: that the entire means of production of a nation, indeed of the whole world economy, become the private property of a single person or joint-stock company, which disposes over them at will; that the whole economic mechanism is concentrated into a single enormous enterprise, in which everything has to serve a single master and belongs to a single master. Private property in the means of production leads, in capitalist society, to the point where all are propertyless, one single person excepted. It thus leads to its own abolition, to the propertylessness of all and the enslavement of all. But the development of capitalist commodity production thereby also leads to the abolition of its own foundations. Capitalist exploitation becomes absurd if the exploiter no longer finds any other purchasers of his commodities than those whom he exploits. If the wage labourers are the sole consumers, then the products in which the surplus value is embodied are unsaleable, and this surplus value becomes worthless.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Indeed, a condition such as the one described here would be as monstrous as it is impossible. It never will and never can come to that. For the mere approach to this condition must drive the sufferings, antagonisms and contradictions in society to such a height that they become unbearable, that society goes out of joint and collapses, unless the development is given another direction still earlier. But even though this condition will not in reality be fully attained, we are steering rapidly towards it, faster than most people believe. For while on the one hand the concentration of the individual capitalist enterprises into a few hands goes forward, on the other hand, with the development of the division of labour, as we have seen, there grows the mutual dependence of the apparently independent enterprises upon one another. But this mutual dependence becomes ever more a one-sided dependence of the small capitalists upon the great. Just as most of the apparently independent workers of domestic industry are in fact only wage labourers of the capitalist, so too many apparently independent capitalists are already under the domination of other capitalists; many apparently independent capitalist enterprises are in fact already only branches of an enormous capitalist enterprise. And this dependence of the smaller capitalists upon the great is perhaps increasing even more rapidly than the concentration of the various enterprises in the private property of a few. The economic mechanism among the capitalist nations is today already, in the last resort, dominated and exploited by a few giant capitalists. Its concentration under a few firms is almost merely a matter of form.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>While the economic dependence of the great mass of the population upon the capitalist class increases ever more, so too, within this class itself, there grows the dependence of the majority upon a minority ever decreasing in number but steadily increasing in power and wealth.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But just as little as for the proletarians, artisans, small traders and peasants does dependence bring the capitalists an increase in their <em>security.</em> On the contrary, for them too there holds what holds for all the others: with their dependence there grows at the same time the insecurity of their position. Most of all, of course, for the smaller capitalists. But complete security is today offered even by the largest capital no more.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We have already touched upon some of the causes of the growing insecurity of capitalist enterprises: the sensitivity of the whole apparatus to external disturbances increases; but insofar as the capitalist mode of production sharpens the antagonisms between the various classes and nations, allows the masses that confront one another to swell ever more, and shapes their means of struggle ever more powerfully, it increases the occasions for such disturbances and enlarges the devastations which they cause. The growing productivity of labour not only increases the surplus value that falls to the capitalists, it also increases the quantity of commodities that reach the market and must be sold off by the capitalists. With exploitation, competition too grows, the bitter struggle of all entrepreneurs against all entrepreneurs. And hand in hand with this development goes a constant technical upheaval, incessant new inventions and discoveries, which devalue what exists and make superfluous not merely individual workers, not merely individual machines, but often entire enterprises, indeed entire branches of industry.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>No capitalist can build on the future; none knows with certainty whether he will be able to keep what he has acquired and to bequeath it to his children.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The capitalist class itself splits more and more into two strata: one, ever increasing in number, which has become entirely superfluous for economic life, which has nothing to do but to squander and dissipate the growing masses of surplus value that flow to it, insofar as it does not use them to accumulate new capital. Let one recall what we said in the previous chapter about the position of the educated in present-day society, and one will not be surprised to see that by far the greatest majority of the rich idlers throw their money out of the window only in mindless and coarse amusements. The other stratum of capitalists, those who have not yet become superfluous in their enterprises, decreases ever more in number, but their cares and the burdens of their responsibility grow. If the one part of the capitalists sinks ever more into idle loafing, the other wears itself out in the never-resting competitive struggle.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But for both strata the insecurity of their existence grows. Thus the present-day mode of production does not allow even the exploiters, not even those who monopolise all its enormous advantages and pocket them for themselves alone, to come to a full enjoyment of them.<br/> <a id="ch3-t8" name="ch3-t8"> </a></p>
```

## 8. Economic Crises

```{=html}
<p class="fst">As great as the general insecurity for all classes already is under ordinary conditions, it is heightened still enormously by the <em>crises</em> which commodity production, from a certain stage of development onward, calls forth from time to time with natural necessity.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Given the importance which the crises have attained in the last decades for our entire economic life, and the lack of clarity that still prevails in wide circles concerning their causes, it is surely permissible to go into the matter somewhat more closely.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The great modern crises which shake the world market spring from <em>overproduction,</em> which in turn is a consequence of the <em>planlessness</em> that is necessarily bound up with commodity production.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>An overproduction in the sense that one produces more than one needs can occur under any mode of production. But it can of course do no harm if the producers produce for their own needs. If, for example, a primitive peasant family harvests more grain than it needs, then it stores the surplus for times of crop failure, or, if its barns are overfull, it feeds it to the cattle, or, in the worst case, it simply lets it lie.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is otherwise with commodity production. This presupposes (in its developed form) that no one produces for himself, everyone for others. Everyone must buy what he needs. But the total production is by no means planned out systematically; rather, it is left to each producer to guess how great the demand is for the commodities he produces. On the other hand, under commodity production, as soon as it has advanced beyond the lowest stage of barter, no one can buy — apart from the producers of the money commodity, the precious metals — before he has sold. These are the two roots from which the <em>crisis</em> springs.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Let us take the simplest case for illustration. In a market there come together a possessor of money, say a gold digger, with twenty marks of gold; further, a vintner with a small cask of wine, a linen weaver with a piece of linen, and a miller with a sack of flour. Let each of these commodities have the same value of twenty marks — a different assumption would merely make the case more complicated, without altering anything in the final result. Let these four commodity-owners be the only ones in the market. Let us now assume that each has correctly calculated the needs of the other: the vintner sells his wine to the gold digger and, with the twenty marks he receives for it, buys the piece of linen from the linen weaver; the latter, finally, uses the proceeds from his linen to acquire the sack of flour. Everyone goes home from the market satisfied.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>A year later the four come together again; each counts on the same sale as before. The possessor of money likewise does not scorn the vintner's wine. But the vintner unfortunately has no need for linen; or he needs the money perhaps to pay a debt and therefore prefers to go about in a torn shirt rather than buy linen. The vintner keeps his twenty marks in his pocket and goes home. The linen weaver now waits in vain for a buyer. And since the linen weaver waits, the miller waits too. Though the weaver's family is very hungry, though he longs for the sack of flour, he has produced linen for which there is no demand, and because the linen was superfluous, the flour has now also become superfluous. Weaver and miller have no money, they cannot buy what they need; and what they have produced is now overproduced, and likewise also what has been produced for them, for example, to continue the example, the table which the joiner expected the miller would buy.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The most essential phenomena of an economic crisis are already given in this example. Naturally it does not in reality occur under such simple conditions. In the beginnings of commodity production every enterprise still produces more or less for its own use; commodity production forms in each family merely a part of its entire production. The linen weaver and the miller of our example each possess a piece of land and some livestock and can wait a while with equanimity until a buyer is found for their commodities. In case of need they can also live without him.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But the market too is still small in the beginnings of commodity production, easily surveyable, and production and consumption, the whole social life, move year in, year out in the same track. In the small communities of former times everyone knew the other, his needs, his purchasing capacity, quite well. The economic mechanism always remained the same; the number of producers, the productivity of their labour, the quantity of their products, the number of consumers, their needs, the sums of money at their disposal — all these conditions changed only slowly, and every change was at once noticed and taken into account.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Things took a different shape through the rise of <em>commodity trade.</em> Under its influence, production for one's own needs recedes ever further, the individual commodity producers and still more the commodity dealers become ever more exclusively dependent on the sale of their commodities, and indeed on the most rapid possible sale. A delay or even a prevention of the sale of a commodity now becomes ever more fateful for its owner; under certain circumstances it can lead to his economic ruin. At the same time, however, the possibilities of a stagnation in trade also grow.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Through commodity trade the most various markets, lying far apart from one another, are brought into connection with each other; the total market is thereby greatly enlarged, but also made less surveyable. This is further promoted by the interposing of one or more middlemen between producers and consumers, as trade brings with it. At the same time the commodities become more mobile through trade and the development of the transport system; a slight stimulus suffices to make them stream together in great quantity at one point.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The estimation of the demand and of the existing stocks of commodities now becomes ever more uncertain; the development of statistics does not remove this uncertainty, it only makes it possible to make an estimation at all, which from a certain height of commodity production onward would be impossible without statistics. More and more the entire economic life becomes dependent on commercial speculation, and this becomes ever more daring.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The merchant is a speculator from the outset; speculation was not first invented on the stock exchange. And speculation is a necessary function of the capitalist. In speculating—that is, in estimating the demand to be expected—in buying his commodities where they are cheap, that is, present in abundance, and selling them where they are dear, that is, scarce, the merchant helps to bring some order into the chaos of the planless production of the private enterprises that are independent of one another. But in his speculations he may also deceive himself. All the more so since he has no time for long reflection, for he is not the only merchant in the world. Hundreds and thousands of competitors lie in wait to exploit every favourable opportunity just as he does; whoever spies it first derives the greatest advantage from it. So it is a matter of being quick, of not deliberating much, of not asking around at length, but of daring: nothing ventured, nothing gained! But he can also lose. If somewhere on a market there is a great demand for a commodity, then it soon streams there in masses, until more of it is present than the market can digest. Then prices fall, and the merchant must sell cheaply, often at a loss, or seek out another, better market with his commodities. His losses in this game can be so great that they ruin him.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Under the rule of a developed commodity production there are always either too few or too many commodities present on a market; the bourgeois economists declare this to be a most wise and admirable arrangement; we think somewhat differently; but in any case it is unavoidable so long as commodity production—from a certain height of development on—exists. This wise arrangement can, however, under certain circumstances also lead to the result that, as a consequence of some unusually strong stimulus, the overloading of a market with commodities becomes an unusually great one, so that accordingly the losses of the merchants too are especially extensive and a large number of them become insolvent—bankrupt. There we already have a commercial crisis in its finest form.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The development of the transport system on the one hand and of the credit system on the other facilitates the sudden flooding of a market with commodities, but thereby also promotes crises and increases their devastating effects.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>All the same, commercial crises were bound to be limited in extent and depth so long as small-scale enterprise constituted the predominant form of production. It was not possible for the quantity of products produced for the <em>total market</em> to swell rapidly under the influence of any stimulus. Under the rule of the artisan small-scale enterprise, production is not capable of rapid expansion. It cannot be enlarged by increasing the number of workers, since in ordinary times it in any case employs all the able-bodied members of the strata of the population devoted to it. It can only be enlarged by extending the workload of the individual—lengthening the working time, Sunday labour, and so on. But for that the independent artisan and peasant in the good old days, when he did not yet have to withstand the competition of the large-scale enterprise, had devilishly little inclination. And even if he did bring himself to overwork, it did not help much, for the productivity of labour was slight.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This changes with the rise of capitalist large-scale industry. It not only develops all the aids that enable commerce to flood the markets rapidly with commodities to a formerly undreamt-of efficiency, it not only expands the market into a world market embracing the whole globe, it not only increases the number of intermediary links between producers and consumers, it also enables production to follow every stimulus of commerce and to expand by leaps and bounds.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The very circumstance that the workers are now wholly subjected to the capitalist, that he can increase their working hours, abolish their Sunday rest, their night's rest, puts the capitalists in a position to expand production more rapidly than was previously possible. But one hour of extra labour means today, given the great productivity of labour, an entirely different expansion of production than in the times of the artisan trade. The capitalists are, moreover, also able to expand their enterprises rapidly. Capital is a very elastic, extensible magnitude, notably thanks to the credit system. A brisk course of business increases confidence, lures money out into the street, shortens the circulation time of a part of the capital, thus increasing its effectiveness, and so on. But the most important thing is: <em>an industrial reserve army of workers is always at the disposal of capital.</em> Thus the capitalist is able at any time to enlarge his enterprise, to hire new workers, to raise production rapidly, to exploit properly every favourable state of the market.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>At the beginning of this chapter we explained that under the rule of large-scale industry industrial capital comes more and more to the fore and dominates the whole capitalist machinery ever more. Within capitalist industry itself, however, particular branches of industry in turn become leading ones, notably the <em>textile</em> and <em>iron industries.</em> If one of these receives a special impetus, for example through the opening of a new large market, say China, or through the sudden undertaking of extensive railway construction, say in America, then it not only expands rapidly but immediately communicates the impetus it has received to the whole economic life. The capitalists enlarge their enterprises, establish new ones, increase the use of raw and auxiliary materials; new workers are hired, and at the same time ground rents, profits, and wages grow. The demand for the most diverse commodities rises, the most diverse industries begin to take part in the economic upswing, which finally becomes a general one. Every enterprise seems bound to flourish, confidence becomes blind, credit unmeasured; whoever has any money at all seeks to invest it profitably, whoever has a share in the soaring rents and profits seeks to transform some of it into capital. The intoxication of bliss is a general one.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Meanwhile production has expanded enormously, the additional demand of the market has been satisfied; but production continues. For one knows nothing of the other, and even if in sober moments doubts may arise in this or that capitalist, they are stifled by the necessity of exploiting the favourable state of the market, of not lagging behind in the competitive chase. The hindmost is bitten by the dogs. The sale of the overproduced commodities proceeds ever more heavily and slowly, the warehouses of the commercial houses fill up, but the intoxication continues. Then one of the commercial houses is supposed to pay for the commodities that it took from the manufacturer on credit months ago. The commodities are still unsold; it possesses the commodities, but not money; it cannot fulfil its obligations, it is bankrupt. But the manufacturer likewise has payments to make; since his debtor cannot pay, he too is finished. One bankruptcy follows another. General consternation sets in; in place of blind confidence there comes equally blind fear, the panic is general, the crash is complete.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The whole of economic life is shaken to its very foundations. Every enterprise that is not firmly rooted in the ground collapses. Ruin strikes not only the fraudulent enterprises, but also all those which in ordinary times could only just barely keep their heads above water; in times of crisis the expropriation of the peasants, artisans and small capitalists is carried out most rapidly. But many of the great ones fall too, and none is safe from being swept along in the general collapse. Rich booty, to be sure, flows to those among the great capitalists who remain standing; in times of crisis not only the expropriation of the “little people,” but also the concentration of enterprises in few hands and the expansion of great fortunes proceed more easily than at other times.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But no one knows whether he will remain standing, whether he will outlast the crisis; and during the crisis, until the general course of business has more or less returned to its accustomed track, all the horrors of the present mode of production are driven to their highest pitch; insecurity, distress, prostitution and crime increase. Thousands starve and freeze to death because they have created too many foodstuffs, too many clothes, too many dwellings. It is here that it becomes most glaringly apparent that the present productive forces are becoming ever more incompatible with commodity production, that private property in the means of production is becoming ever more a curse, above all for the propertyless, but ultimately also for the propertied.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Some economists expect the <em>cartels</em> to bring about the elimination of crises. Nothing could be more mistaken!</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>A regulation of production by the cartels presupposes above all that they embrace all the important branches of production and are built up on an <em>international basis</em>, extending over all the countries of the capitalist mode of production. So far there does not exist a single international cartel in any of the branches of industry that are decisive for the whole of economic life. International cartels are very difficult to form and equally difficult to hold together. More than forty years ago Marx already observed that not only does competition create monopoly, but monopoly also creates competition. The greater the profits which accrue to a series of enterprises from a cartel, the greater the danger that an outside powerful capitalist will attempt to snatch these profits from them by founding a competing enterprise; and the better the course of business, the greater the temptation for each individual member of the cartel to withdraw from the restrictions on production which the cartel imposes. When prices are falling the striving for cartelisation is very great; when prices are rising every industrialist seeks to exploit the conjuncture as fully as possible and to throw as much commodity as possible onto the market. As a barrier against overproduction the cartels will as a rule fail in their service. Their principal task with regard to overproduction consists not in preventing it, but in shifting its consequences from the capitalists onto the workers and the consumers. They are meant to help the great capitalists to get through the crisis, to restrict production temporarily, to dismiss workers, and so on, without the profit suffering thereby.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But let us for once assume the improbable, that it should succeed within the foreseeable future in organising the great world industries into cartels which are international and so strictly disciplined that they hold together even under the most favourable course of business. What would be the consequence? Competition between the capitalists of the same branch of industry would thereby, in the best case, be abolished only in <em>one</em> direction. It would lead us too far afield to investigate what consequences the other sides of competition, which remain in existence, must entail. Let only <em>one</em> point be kept in view: the more competition among the entrepreneurs of the same branch of industry disappears, the greater the antagonism between them and the entrepreneurs of the other branches of industry which are dependent on their commodities. If the struggles between the individual producers of the same branch of industry cease, then the struggles between producers and consumers all the more intensify – the latter word taken in the widest sense. In this sense, however, every producer is also a consumer; the cotton-spinner, for example, quite apart from his personal consumption, is a consumer of cotton, coal, machines, oil, and so on. The whole capitalist class will no longer break up into individual persons, but into strata which combat one another most bitterly.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Today every capitalist has the striving to produce as much as possible, to bring <em>as much commodity as possible</em> onto the market; for the more commodity, the more profit – other things being equal. Only his calculations of the market's capacity to absorb, and of course the extent of his capital, set a barrier to his production. By contrast, when cartelisation becomes general, we do not get a regulation of production and thereby a cessation of crises, as some whitewashers depict to us, but rather we get the general striving of every cartel to produce <em>as little as possible</em>, for the smaller the quantity of commodities, the higher the prices. The earlier practice of merchants, when the market was overstocked, of destroying a part of the existing commodities in order to obtain profitable prices for the remainder, will then become the general practice. That society cannot survive under such conditions is clear. If every cartel strives for underproduction, then, on the other hand, each must strive to compel the other cartels, whose commodities it needs, into overproduction. There are many ways of doing this. The simplest is to restrict one's own consumption even more than the other cartel restricts its production. Another is to call upon science to supply a substitute for the commodity whose production has been restricted. A third consists in the consumers concerned setting about themselves to produce what they need.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Let us assume, for example, that the copper mines form a cartel, restrict the production of copper and drive its prices up. What is the consequence? Of the industrialists whose enterprises process copper, some will shut down their operations until better times, some will seek to use other metals in place of copper; others again will themselves acquire or put into operation copper mines and thus make themselves independent of the copper ring. The end is the bursting apart and the bankruptcy of this cartel, <em>hence a crisis.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Something similar, as is well known, has already taken place in reality.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The cartels therefore do not banish crises from the world. If they should have any success in this respect, it could at most be that the crises take on another form – but no better one. The bankruptcies will not cease; the difference will only be that they become more extensive, that they strike not merely individual capitalists, but always whole strata of capitalists at once, and with these of course also the whole great mass of the existences dependent upon them. The cartels cannot eliminate crises, but they can indeed cause crises which are far more devastating than anything we have hitherto seen.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Only then, when all the cartels had merged into a single one, in whose hands the entire means of production of all capitalist nations were united, that is, when private property in the means of production had in fact been abolished, only then could cartelisation make possible an elimination of the crises. On the other hand, from a certain level of economic development onward the crises are unavoidable so long as private property in the means of production exists. It is not feasible one-sidedly to abolish merely the dark sides of private property, while allowing this property itself to continue existing as before.<br/> <a id="ch3-t9" name="ch3-t9"> </a></p>
```

## 9. Chronic Overproduction

```{=html}
<p class="fst">Alongside the periodic crises, alongside the temporary overproduction with the subsequent temporary destruction of value and squandering of energy, there develops ever more strongly the <em>permanent (chronic) overproduction</em> and the <em>permanent squandering of energy.</em> We have seen that the technical revolution proceeds uninterruptedly; its scope becomes ever more extensive, for from year to year new branches of industry, new regions are conquered by large-scale capitalist production; the productivity of labour therefore grows unceasingly, and indeed (taking the totality of capitalist societies) ever more rapidly. At the same time the accumulation of new capital proceeds uninterruptedly.
```
