The more the exploitation of the individual worker and the number of exploited workers (not merely in one country, but in all countries exploited by capital) grows, the more the mass of surplus value grows as well, the greater becomes the mass of wealth that the capitalist class can set aside each year in order to transform it into capital. Capitalist production therefore cannot come to a halt at a certain magnitude; its steady expansion and the steady expansion of its market is a vital question for it; standstill is its death. Whereas formerly the handicraft and the peasantry of a country produced the same amount year in, year out, and production as a rule grew only with the population, the capitalist mode of production requires from the outset uninterrupted growth of production; every check to it means a wasting sickness for society, which becomes the more painful and unbearable the longer it lasts. Alongside the temporary incentives to the expansion of production, which are caused by temporary expansions of the market, we find a permanent urge to the expansion of production, which arises from the relations of production themselves, and which, instead of being caused by an expansion of the market, rather makes a steady expansion of it necessary.</p>

```{=html}
<p>But over the last two decades this expansion has no longer wished to proceed properly; that is to say, it still takes place, but while production has the tendency to expand ever more rapidly, the expansion of the market proceeds ever more slowly.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>To be sure, the territory over which capitalist production can extend its market is an immense one; it leaps over all local and national barriers, it may make the whole globe its market. But it has made the globe very small. As recently as a hundred years ago, apart from the western parts of Europe, only various coastal lands and islands in the foreign continents formed the market for the capitalist industry that was carried on chiefly in England. So enormous, however, was the energy and greed of the capitalists and their champions and accomplices, and so gigantic the means at their disposal, that since then almost all the countries of the earth have been opened up to the wares of the capitalist industry — no longer merely English, but pan-European — so that there remain to be opened up scarcely any markets other than those in which there is not much more to be got than fever and — blows.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The astonishing development of the transport system does indeed make possible, from year to year, an ever better exploitation of every market, but precisely among those peoples who are not entirely savages, who have developed a certain culture, certain cultural needs, the market takes on ever more a different appearance. The penetration of the wares of large-scale capitalist industry kills off the native small-scale enterprises everywhere, not merely in Europe, and transforms the artisans and peasants into proletarians. This brings about two important changes in each of the sales markets of capitalist industry. It reduces the purchasing power of the population and thus works against the expansion of sales in the markets concerned. But it also creates there — and this is far more important — through the production of a proletariat, the foundations for the introduction of the capitalist mode of production. European large-scale industry thus digs its own grave. From a certain point of development onwards, every further expansion of the market means for it the emergence of a new competitor. The large-scale industry of the United States, which counts scarcely a single generation, is proceeding not merely to make itself entirely independent of the European, but also to lay claim to the whole of America for itself; the still younger Russian industry is beginning to supply, single-handed, the whole immense territory that Russia dominates in Europe and Asia with industrial wares; the East Indies, China, Japan, Australia are developing into industrial states which sooner or later will be able to satisfy themselves in industrial respects; in short, the moment seems to be near when the market of European industry will not only no longer expand, but will begin to contract. But that would mean nothing other than the bankruptcy of the whole capitalist society.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Meanwhile, for some time now the expansion of the market has been proceeding far too slowly for the needs of capitalist production; the latter finds more and more checks, it becomes ever more impossible for it to unfold its productive forces fully. The times of economic upswing become ever shorter, the times of crisis ever longer, overproduction becomes ever more the permanent condition of production.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>As a consequence there grows the quantity of means of production that are not sufficiently exploited or not exploited at all, the quantity of riches that are lost unused, the quantity of labour-power that must remain lying fallow. To these are to be reckoned not merely the throngs of the unemployed, who have already grown into a menacing social danger, but also all those innumerable and ever-increasing parasites on the body of society who, because they cannot be productively active, seek to eke out a wretched existence through the most varied, mostly quite superfluous, but often most exhausting activities — the petty middlemen, publicans, agents, brokers; to these belongs, furthermore, the whole immense mass of lumpenproletarians in the most varied gradations, the higher and lower charlatans, the criminal element, professional prostitution with its pimps and other existences that cling to it; to these belong, furthermore, the numerous throngs of those who hire themselves out to the propertied for personal services; finally the great mass of soldiers: the steady growth of the armies over the last twenty years would scarcely have been possible without the overproduction that allowed industry to dispense with so much labour-power.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Capitalist society is beginning to suffocate in its own abundance; it becomes ever less capable of enduring the full unfolding of the productive forces that it has created. Ever more productive forces must lie fallow, ever more products must be uselessly squandered, if it is not to run wholly out of control.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The capitalist mode of production, the replacement of small-scale enterprise by the capitalist large-scale enterprise, whose means of production are united as private property in a few hands, whose workers are propertyless proletarians — this mode of production was the means of increasing infinitely the extremely limited productive power of labour that was peculiar to the handicraft and to peasant agriculture. To accomplish this was the world-historical task of the capitalist class. It has accomplished the solution of this task by inflicting terrible suffering upon the masses of the people whom it expropriated and exploited, but it has accomplished it. It was just as much a historical necessity as the two foundations from which it sprang: commodity production and the private property in the means of production and products that is closely bound up with it.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But if it and its foundations were historically necessary, they are so no longer today. The functions of the capitalist class fall more and more to salaried employees; the great majority of capitalists have only the task of consuming what others produce; the capitalist has become just as superfluous as the feudal lord was a hundred years ago.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Indeed, still more. Like the feudal nobility in the last century, the capitalist class today is already an obstacle to further development. The private property in the means of production has long since ceased to guarantee to every producer the property in his products and his freedom. Today it is rapidly moving towards abolishing this property and this freedom for the entire population of the capitalist nations; from a foundation of society it becomes more and more a means of dissolving all the foundations of society. And from a means of spurring society on to the most rapid unfolding of its productive forces, it has transformed itself into a means that increasingly compels society to squander and leave idle its productive forces.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Thus the private property in the means of production transforms its original essence into its opposite, not merely for the producers of small-scale enterprises, but for the whole of society. From a driving force of social development it becomes a cause of social stagnation, of social bankruptcy.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Today it is no longer a question of whether one wishes to maintain the private property in the means of production or not. Its downfall is certain. The only question is: <em>Shall it drag society with it into the abyss, or shall society rid itself of this ruinous burden, in order to be able to march on, free and newly strengthened, along the path that the laws of development prescribe to it?</em></p>
```

## Notes

```{=html}
<p class="note"><a href="#ch3-f7" id="ch3-n7" name="ch3-n7">7.</a> Strictly speaking, rent of tenancy and ground-rent are not the same thing. In the rent of tenancy there is usually also a portion of capital interest. For our purposes here, however, one may equate rent of tenancy and ground-rent.</p>
```


---

[↑ Return to Table of Contents](../index.qmd)
